era_context: Mid-19th century: Industrial Revolution transforming Western economies; European revolutions sweeping the continent; United States expanding westward and approaching sectional crisis; China weakened by internal strife and foreign pressure; Latin America consolidating post-independence states. This is an era of ideological ferment, nationalist awakening, and imperial expansion.

events.title: Revolutions of 1848 sweep across Europe
events.description: A wave of revolutionary movements swept across Europe in 1848, driven by nationalist sentiment, liberal demands for constitutional government, and social discontent. The revolutions began in France in February 1848 with the overthrow of King Louis-Philippe and the establishment of the Second French Republic. The revolutionary ferment spread rapidly to the German states, the Austrian Empire, Italy, and other regions. The revolutions demanded national unification, constitutional government, and social reform. Although most of the revolutions were suppressed by 1849, they represented a major challenge to the conservative order established by the Congress of Vienna and had lasting effects on European political culture and ideology.

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts and official government records
Sperber, J. (1994). Rhineland Radicals: The Democratic Movement and the Revolution of 1848–1849

events.certainty_note: The revolutions of 1848 are extensively documented in European sources. The timing and progression are well-established.

events.title: California Gold Rush begins
events.description: Gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill in Coloma, California, on 24 January 1848 by James W. Marshall. The discovery, initially kept secret, gradually became known, and by late 1848, prospectors were beginning to arrive in California. The discovery would trigger the massive California Gold Rush of 1849–1855, transforming California's economy and demographics. The gold discovery accelerated California's development and contributed to sectional tensions in the United States over the status of new territories acquired in the Mexican-American War.

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts and Marshall's own accounts
Brands, H. W. (2002). The Age of Gold: The California Gold Rush and the New American Dream

events.certainty_note: The discovery on 24 January 1848 is well-documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ends Mexican-American War
events.description: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, signed on 2 February 1848, formally ended the Mexican-American War (1846–1848). Under the treaty, Mexico ceded approximately 55% of its territory to the United States, including present-day California, Nevada, Utah, most of Arizona and New Mexico, and parts of Colorado and Wyoming. In exchange, the United States paid Mexico $15 million and assumed $3.25 million in claims by American citizens against Mexico. The treaty also guaranteed property rights and citizenship options for Mexicans living in the ceded territories. The acquisition of this vast territory intensified sectional tensions in the United States over the expansion of slavery and contributed to the political crisis that would lead to the Civil War.

sources.name: Treaty text and contemporary diplomatic records
Foner, E. (1970). The Free Soil, Free Labor, Free Men: The Ideology of the Republican Party Before the Civil War

events.certainty_note: The treaty was signed on 2 February 1848 and is extensively documented.

events.title: Communist Manifesto published
events.description: The Communist Manifesto, written by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, was published in February 1848 in German. The manifesto outlined the principles of scientific socialism and called for the overthrow of capitalist society through proletarian revolution. Published at the height of the European revolutions of 1848, the manifesto became one of the most influential political documents of the 19th and 20th centuries. Although it had limited immediate impact, it would become the foundational text for communist and socialist movements worldwide and profoundly influenced political ideology and practice.

sources.name: Hobsbawm, E. J. (1996). The Age of Extremes: A History of the World, 1914–1991

events.certainty_note: The publication in February 1848 is well-documented.

events.title: Second Anglo-Sikh War begins
events.description: The Second Anglo-Sikh War began in 1848 when tensions between the British East India Company and the Sikh state of Punjab escalated into open conflict. The war was triggered by disputes over the succession to the Sikh throne and British expansionist ambitions in the Punjab. The conflict lasted until 1849, when the British achieved decisive victory at the Battle of Gujrat. The war resulted in the dissolution of the Sikh state and the incorporation of the Punjab into British India, completing British territorial consolidation in the subcontinent.

sources.name: British East India Company records and official dispatches
Priya, D. (1998). The Sikhs in Indian Politics

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Second Anglo-Sikh War in 1848 is well-documented in British records.

events.title: Sikh Wars: Battle of Ramnagar
events.description: The Battle of Ramnagar, fought on 18 November 1848, was an early engagement of the Second Anglo-Sikh War. The British forces, commanded by Lord Gough, engaged the Sikh army in a tactical engagement that demonstrated the growing military superiority of the British. Although the battle was not decisively won by either side, it demonstrated British military capability and contributed to the eventual British victory in the war.

sources.name: British military dispatches and official reports
Priya, D. (1998). The Sikhs in Indian Politics

events.certainty_note: The date and outcome of the Battle of Ramnagar are well-documented in British military records.

disconfirming_evidence: The revolutions of 1848 are sometimes presented as a unified movement for liberal democracy and national unification, but modern scholarship emphasises the diversity of the revolutionary movements and the ways in which different groups (liberals, socialists, nationalists) had conflicting goals. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo is sometimes presented as a straightforward territorial acquisition, but it involved the displacement of Mexican populations and the imposition of American sovereignty over territories with established Mexican settlements. The Communist Manifesto is sometimes presented as having immediate revolutionary impact, but its actual influence was limited in 1848 and only grew significantly later.
historiographic_note: 1848 is very well-documented in Western sources, particularly European and American records. The revolutions are extensively covered in contemporary newspapers and official records. Indian sources on the Sikh Wars are less comprehensive than British records. The perspectives of colonised peoples, enslaved people, and working-class people are underrepresented in surviving sources, though labour and socialist movements are documented in some detail.

graph_edges.note: The revolutions of 1848 were suppressed in 1849.
The gold discovery in 1848 led to the acceleration of the gold rush in 1849.
The territorial acquisition from the Mexican-American War contributed to the sectional tensions addressed in the Compromise of 1850.
The Second Anglo-Sikh War that began in 1848 concluded with British victory in 1849.
